original_hypothesis.title: RewardsDistributor Permanent Token Loss from checkpoint_token 20-Iteration Cap with WEEK=1800
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: RewardsDistributor._checkpoint_token() distributes incoming tokens across weekly periods using a loop capped at 20 iterations. With WEEK=1800 seconds, 20 iterations covers only 36,000 seconds (10 hours). If checkpoint_token is not called for >10 hours, the loop exits without distributing tokens to the missed periods. The undistributed tokens remain in the contract but are never assigned to any tokens_per_week slot, making them permanently unclaimable by users.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: checkpoint_token can only be called by the depositor role. A 10-hour gap due to bot downtime, network congestion, or operational failure permanently loses rebase tokens from distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = 0; i < 20; i++) { next_week = this_week + WEEK; ... } — if 20 iterations exhausted before reaching block.timestamp, remaining to_distribute is unassigned

preconditions: checkpoint_token not called for >10 hours (20 * 1800 seconds)
Tokens have been deposited to RewardsDistributor during the gap

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: 20 iterations * 1800s = 36,000s (10h) max coverage; tokens arriving during gaps beyond 10h never enter tokens_per_week
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Only depositor can call checkpoint_token; no catch-up mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any >10 hour gap in checkpoint_token calls

operational_questions: What is the current checkpoint_token call frequency?
Has there been any >10 hour gap since deployment?
Is there automated infrastructure calling checkpoint_token?
validation_requirements: Simulate 12-hour gap and verify token distribution
Check if missed tokens are recoverable via subsequent calls

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Depositor bot experiences downtime for >10 hours
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Tokens arrive at RewardsDistributor during downtime
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When checkpoint_token resumes, loop caps at 20 iterations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Tokens for missed periods are never distributed — permanently stuck
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — operational failure triggers this automatically

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Permanent loss of rebase tokens from distribution
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users receive less rebase than entitled
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Lost tokens cannot be reassigned; only withdrawERC20 by owner can extract them

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Gap between last_token_time and block.timestamp exceeding 10 hours
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Reduced rebase claims after service interruptions

validation_result.mechanism: 20-iteration cap token loss with short WEEK
validation_result.impact_summary: Any gap >10 hours between checkpoint_token calls causes a proportional fraction of deposited rebase tokens to be permanently unassigned to any tokens_per_week slot, making them unclaimable by users.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _checkpoint_token() function distributes incoming tokens (to_distribute) across weekly time slots proportionally. The loop is capped at 20 iterations. With WEEK=1800 seconds, 20 iterations cover only 36,000 seconds (10 hours). If the gap between calls exceeds 10 hours, the loop exhausts all 20 iterations without reaching block.timestamp. The proportional distribution formula allocates to_distribute * (next_week - t) / since_last per period, where since_last is the total gap. After 20 iterations covering 20*WEEK time, only to_distribute * (20 * WEEK) / since_last tokens are distributed. The remainder (to_distribute * (since_last - 20*WEEK) / since_last) is never assigned to any tokens_per_week slot. Meanwhile, token_last_balance is set to the full contract balance at the start of the function, so the undistributed tokens are permanently trapped - they're tracked in token_last_balance but never in tokens_per_week, making them inaccessible via claim().
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows: (1) WEEK = 1800 in BlackTimeLibrary.sol line 4, (2) loop cap of 20 in RewardsDistributor.sol line 60, (3) token_last_balance set to full balance before the loop at line 52, (4) proportional distribution within the loop that cannot cover >36000 seconds of gap. The combination of these four code facts confirms the defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When the loop exhausts 20 iterations (covering only 20*1800=36000 seconds=10 hours), the function exits without distributing the remaining proportional share of to_distribute. token_last_balance was already set to the full balance at line 52 (before the loop), so the undistributed portion is permanently lost from the distribution system. It remains in the contract but can never be claimed by users.

WEEK is 1800 seconds (30 minutes) instead of a traditional 604800 seconds (7 days). The _checkpoint_token loop cap of 20 was likely designed for 7-day weeks (covering 140 days), but with 1800-second weeks it only covers 10 hours.

calling_sequence: 5. to_distribute = token_balance - token_last_balance calculates new tokens
6. token_last_balance = token_balance updates tracking (BEFORE distribution loop)
7. Loop iterates up to 20 times, each advancing by WEEK=1800 seconds
8. If gap > 36000 seconds (10 hours), loop exhausts without reaching block.timestamp
9. Proportional tokens for periods beyond iteration 20 are never assigned to tokens_per_week
10. last_token_time = block.timestamp ensures next call starts fresh, losing the undistributed amount
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability lies in the interplay between line 52 (token_last_balance = token_balance, set BEFORE the loop) and the loop cap at line 60 (i < 20). When the loop exits after 20 iterations at line 60 without hitting the break at line 63, the proportional share of to_distribute for uncovered periods is never written to tokens_per_week. On the next checkpoint call, to_distribute will only capture NEW deposits (since token_last_balance already accounts for the full balance), so the lost tokens from the previous gap can never be recovered through normal operation.

deployment_assumptions_checked: WEEK = 1800 seconds is hardcoded in BlackTimeLibrary.sol, not a deployment parameter
config_validation.deployment_evidence: WEEK is a compile-time constant in BlackTimeLibrary.sol (line 4: uint256 internal constant WEEK = 1800). No deployment scripts configure this value.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an adversarial attack but an operational failure mode. The scenario: (1) Depositor bot goes offline or is delayed for >10 hours, (2) During this time, tokens are deposited to RewardsDistributor by the minter, (3) When checkpoint_token is called after the >10h gap, the 20-iteration loop cannot cover the full gap, (4) A fraction equal to (gap_seconds - 36000) / gap_seconds of the to_distribute amount is permanently lost from distribution. For example, a 20-hour gap loses 50% of deposited tokens. A 30-hour gap loses 67%. The tokens remain in the contract but are never assigned to tokens_per_week slots, making them unclaimable. The owner can recover via withdrawERC20 but this is manual intervention, not automatic recovery.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL severity. With WEEK=1800 seconds, even moderate operational delays (>10 hours) permanently remove tokens from the distribution system. The lost tokens inflate token_last_balance relative to sum(tokens_per_week), creating a permanent solvency gap in the rewards system. Over time, if gaps occur repeatedly, significant rebase rewards could be lost. The only recovery is manual intervention by the owner using withdrawERC20.
recommended_mitigations: Increase the loop cap from 20 to a value that covers reasonable operational gaps. With WEEK=1800, a cap of 500 would cover ~10.4 days.
Alternatively, implement a multi-call catch-up mechanism where _checkpoint_token can be called multiple times in succession to cover arbitrarily long gaps.

Add a safety check: if the loop exhausts without reaching block.timestamp, revert or store the remaining undistributed amount for the next call rather than setting token_last_balance to the full balance before the loop.
Move the token_last_balance update to AFTER the distribution loop, accounting only for actually distributed tokens.
Consider increasing WEEK to a more standard duration to reduce the frequency requirement for checkpoint_token calls.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy RewardsDistributor with a mock VotingEscrow. 2. Call checkpoint_token at time T. 3. Transfer 1000 tokens to the contract. 4. Advance time by 15 hours (54000 seconds, exceeding 20*1800=36000). 5. Call checkpoint_token. 6. Sum all tokens_per_week values from T to T+54000 (in WEEK increments). 7. Verify the sum is less than 1000 (specifically ~667 tokens). 8. Verify the remaining ~333 tokens are stuck in the contract (balance > sum of future claimable amounts).
uncertainties: The operational frequency of checkpoint_token calls in production is unknown - if called every <10 hours reliably, the issue doesn't manifest in normal operation
The owner's withdrawERC20 function provides a manual recovery path, but it requires awareness of the issue and doesn't restore proper distribution to users
additional_testing_needed: Verify the actual checkpoint_token call frequency in production via on-chain analysis
Quantify cumulative token loss from historical gaps exceeding 10 hours
Test whether multiple rapid checkpoint_token calls after a gap can recover any lost tokens (they cannot based on code analysis, but empirical verification is valuable)
